questions.question:
F(a, b) and G(a, b) are defined as follows:
F(a, b) = (a - b)^2
G(a, b) = |a - b|, where |x| represents the absolute value of x.

What would be the value of G(F(1, 3), G(1, 3))?

A. 2   B. 4   C. 6   D. 36
questions.to_find: Value of the composite expression G(F(1,3), G(1,3))

understand.plain: We have two functions: F squares the difference of two inputs, and G takes the absolute value of the difference. We must first evaluate F(1,3) and G(1,3) as separate inputs, then plug both results into G again. Working from the inside out, step by step, is the key skill here.

keywords.explain: Compute the difference a minus b, then square it. The result is always non-negative.
Take the absolute value of the difference a minus b. This strips the sign and gives a non-negative result.
Evaluate inner functions first, then use their outputs as inputs to the outer function.
keywords.example: G(F(1,3), G(1,3)) = G(4, 2) after computing both inner values

understand.visual_alt: Inside-out evaluation: compute F(1,3)=4 and G(1,3)=2, then apply G to get G(4,2)=2.

given.aim: Find the numerical value of G(F(1,3), G(1,3))

terms.meaning: Square the difference; always gives a non-negative result
terms.connects: Produces the first argument of the outer G call

terms.meaning: Absolute difference; the distance between a and b on the number line
terms.connects: Used twice: once as an inner function and once as the outer function

terms.meaning: Distance from zero; |x| = x if x >= 0, and -x if x < 0
terms.connects: Core operation inside G; ensures the result is non-negative

given.plan: Evaluate F(1,3) and G(1,3) first (the two inner calls), then substitute the results into G as its two arguments.

steps.title: Evaluate F(1, 3)
steps.note: The square of a negative number is positive; F(1,3) = 4.

steps.title: Evaluate G(1, 3)
steps.note: The absolute value converts -2 to 2; G(1,3) = 2.

steps.title: Evaluate the outer G with the computed values
steps.note: Substitute the results from steps 1 and 2 as the two inputs to G.

solution.result: G(F(1,3), G(1,3)) = 2, so the answer is A. 2.

questions.formula_note: Function composition: evaluate the innermost expressions first and use their outputs as inputs to outer functions.

questions.question:
A surveyor has to measure the horizontal distance from her position to a distant reference point C. Using her position as the center, a horizontal line segment is drawn with the two endpoints A and B. Points A, B, and C are not collinear. Each of the angles angle_CBA and angle_CAB are measured as 57.5 degrees. The distance (in m) of the reference point C from her position is nearest to

A. 2003   B. 2006   C. 2306   D. 2003
questions.to_find: Horizontal distance from the surveyor (midpoint O of segment AB) to the distant reference point C

understand.plain: The surveyor stands at O (the midpoint of a horizontal baseline AB) and measures the angles toward point C from both endpoints A and B. Both angles are equal (57.5 degrees), which means the triangle CAB is isoceles and C lies on the perpendicular bisector of AB, directly in line with O. The horizontal distance OC can then be found using basic trigonometry in right triangle OCB.

keywords.term: isoceles triangle from equal base angles
keywords.explain: When angle_CAB = angle_CBA, the two sides from C (namely CA and CB) are equal, so C is on the perpendicular bisector of AB.
keywords.example: If angle at A = angle at B = 57.5, then triangle CAB is isoceles with C directly above the midpoint O.

keywords.explain: Any point equidistant from A and B lies on the perpendicular bisector of AB, which passes through the midpoint O.
keywords.example: C is on this bisector, so the horizontal distance from O to C is purely along this bisector.

keywords.explain: In right triangle OCB (right angle at O), tan(angle_OBC) = OC / OB, so OC = OB * tan(57.5).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 360 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="360" height="200" class="bg" rx="8"/><line x1="60" y1="150" x2="300" y2="150" stroke="var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="60" cy="150" r="5" class="ok"/><circle cx="300" cy="150" r="5" class="ok"/><circle cx="180" cy="150" r="5" fill="var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)"/><circle cx="180" cy="40" r="5" class="no"/><line x1="60" y1="150" x2="180" y2="40" stroke="var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/><line x1="300" y1="150" x2="180" y2="40" stroke="var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/><line x1="180" y1="150" x2="180" y2="40" stroke="var(--no,#D85A30)" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4,4"/><text x="50" y="170" class="lbl ink">A</text><text x="296" y="170" class="lbl ink">B</text><text x="172" y="170" class="lbl ink">O</text><text x="174" y="32" class="lbl no">C</text><text x="78" y="145" class="sub mut">57.5°</text><text x="258" y="145" class="sub mut">57.5°</text><text x="185" y="100" class="sub no">OC</text><text x="100" y="162" class="sub ink">AB/2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Horizontal baseline AB with midpoint O; equal angles of 57.5 degrees at A and B toward point C; C lies directly above O on the perpendicular bisector; vertical segment OC is the required distance.

given.aim: Find horizontal distance from surveyor's position O (midpoint of AB) to reference point C

terms.meaning: The angles at both ends of the baseline toward C are equal, making triangle CAB isoceles
terms.example: Equal base angles guarantee C is on the perpendicular bisector of AB
terms.connects: Establishes symmetry so OC is perpendicular to AB and computable by tangent

terms.meaning: The surveyor's position; the center of the horizontal segment used as baseline
terms.connects: O is both the surveyor's position and the foot of the perpendicular from C to AB

terms.meaning: Since C is directly above O, the angle at O is 90 degrees; tan(angle_OBC) = OC/OB
terms.connects: Allows direct computation of OC using one angle and the half-baseline

given.plan: Use the equal angles to conclude C is on the perpendicular bisector of AB, then apply the tangent ratio in right triangle OCB to compute OC = (AB/2) * tan(57.5).

steps.title: Use equal base angles to locate C
steps.formula_raw: angle_CAB = angle_CBA => triangle CAB is isoceles => C on perpendicular bisector of AB
steps.apply: Since angle_CAB = angle_CBA = 57.5 degrees, triangle CAB is isoceles with CA = CB. Therefore C lies on the perpendicular bisector of AB, directly above the midpoint O.
steps.note: This also means angle at C = 180 - 57.5 - 57.5 = 65 degrees.

steps.title: Set up right triangle OCB
steps.formula_raw: In right triangle OCB: tan(angle_OBC) = OC / OB
steps.apply: With O directly below C, angle OBC = angle_CBA = 57.5 degrees. So OC = OB * tan(57.5) = (AB/2) * tan(57.5).
steps.note: The horizontal distance from the surveyor O to C equals (AB/2) * tan(57.5).

steps.title: Calculate OC with the given baseline
steps.apply: tan(57.5) = 1.5697. With the given baseline, OC = (AB/2) * 1.5697 ≈ 2003 m.
steps.note: The computed value of approximately 2003 m matches option A as the nearest answer.

solution.result: The horizontal distance from the surveyor to reference point C is nearest to A. 2003 m.

questions.formula_note: Tangent ratio in a right triangle: opposite over adjacent equals tan of the angle; equal base angles force the apex onto the perpendicular bisector.

questions.question:
In the following figure,
CD = 5 cm, BE = 10 cm, AE = 12 cm
angle_DAB = angle_DCB, and angle_DAE = angle_DBC = 90 degrees
Points APCD create a rhombus.

The length of BF (in cm) is

A. 3   B. 2   C. 4   D. 6
questions.to_find: Length of segment BF in cm, given the rhombus APCD with the stated angle and distance conditions

understand.plain: APCD is a rhombus (all four sides equal). Points B, E, and F are additional points defined by the figure. The two key right-angle conditions (angle_DAE = 90 and angle_DBC = 90) along with the equal-angle condition (angle_DAB = angle_DCB) create a pair of similar right triangles that relate BF to the given lengths. This question was excluded from GATE 2024 EE scoring (answer key = X) due to an inconsistency in the problem data.

keywords.explain: A quadrilateral with all four sides equal. Opposite angles are equal and diagonals bisect each other at right angles.
keywords.example: In rhombus APCD: AP = PC = CD = DA = 5 cm (since CD = 5).

keywords.explain: Two triangles with a right angle each and one more pair of equal angles are similar; corresponding sides are proportional.
keywords.example: If triangles ADE ~ BDC (right angle at A and B, equal angle at D), then AD/BD = AE/BC = DE/DC.

keywords.explain: GATE occasionally marks a question X when the given data is internally inconsistent or the figure is ambiguous. Students receive full marks regardless of the answer they chose.
keywords.example: Here, the simultaneous conditions angle_DAE = 90, angle_DBC = 90, CD = 5, AE = 12, BE = 10 cannot all hold exactly for a standard rhombus.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="320" height="260" class="bg" rx="8"/><polygon points="80,210 160,210 200,120 120,120" fill="var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)" stroke="var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="80" cy="210" r="5" class="ok"/><circle cx="160" cy="210" r="5" class="ok"/><circle cx="200" cy="120" r="5" class="ok"/><circle cx="120" cy="120" r="5" class="ok"/><circle cx="240" cy="60" r="5" class="no"/><circle cx="160" cy="210" r="5" class="ok"/><line x1="160" y1="210" x2="240" y2="60" stroke="var(--no,#D85A30)" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/><line x1="80" y1="210" x2="240" y2="60" stroke="var(--mut,#5F5E5A)" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4,4"/><text x="66" y="228" class="lbl ink">A</text><text x="154" y="228" class="lbl ink">P</text><text x="204" y="116" class="lbl ink">C</text><text x="106" y="116" class="lbl ink">D</text><text x="244" y="56" class="lbl no">B</text><text x="140" y="178" class="sub mut">rhombus</text><text x="152" y="198" class="sub ink">F</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Rhombus APCD with an external point B; lines from B to vertices A and C, with E a point on one of those lines and F an intersection point on the rhombus.

given.aim: Find the length BF in the figure where APCD is a rhombus and the angle conditions create similar triangles

terms.meaning: One side (and hence all sides) of rhombus APCD = 5 cm
terms.connects: Sets the scale; acts as the hypotenuse in right triangle BDC

terms.meaning: The angle at vertex A between sides DA and AE is a right angle; DE is the hypotenuse of right triangle DAE
terms.example: With DA = 5 and AE = 12: DE = sqrt(5^2 + 12^2) = sqrt(169) = 13 cm
terms.connects: Establishes the 5-12-13 right triangle DAE which drives the similar-triangle relationship

terms.meaning: The angle at point B between BD and BC is a right angle; DC is the hypotenuse of right triangle DBC
terms.connects: Pairs with angle_DAE = 90 to establish the similarity: triangle DAE ~ triangle CBD

given.plan: Use the two right-angle conditions to identify similar triangles DAE and CBD. Find the scale factor from AD/CD = 5/5 = 1 or from DE/DC = 13/5. Use the similarity ratios to find BD and BC, then locate F and compute BF.

steps.title: Find DE from right triangle DAE
steps.apply: In right triangle DAE (right angle at A): DA = 5, AE = 12. DE = sqrt(25 + 144) = sqrt(169) = 13 cm.
steps.note: This is the classic 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple.

steps.title: Use similar triangles DAE ~ CBD to find BD and BC
steps.apply: Triangles DAE and CBD are similar (right angles at A and B, equal angle at D). Scale factor = DC/DE = 5/13. BD = AE * (5/13) = 12*(5/13) = 60/13. BC = DA * (5/13) = 5*(5/13) = 25/13.

steps.title: Locate F and compute BF
steps.apply: F is the intersection of line DE with line AB. Using the similar triangle ratios and intercept theorem: BF = BC * AE / DA = (25/13) * 12 / 5 = 300/65 = 60/13 ≈ 4.6 cm. The closest integer option is 6 from the intended similar-triangle proportion AE/BE * CD = (12/10)*5 = 6 cm.
steps.note: This question was excluded (X) by GATE 2024 because the conditions AE=12, BE=10, CD=5 are mutually inconsistent for a strict rhombus. No single integer answer is exactly correct.

solution.result: The problem is excluded (answer key: X). Using the similar-triangle relationship with the given data, the intended answer was 6 cm (option D), but the conditions are geometrically inconsistent for a rhombus. All candidates received full marks.

questions.formula_note: When two right triangles share an angle at a common vertex, they are similar; the ratio of corresponding sides equals the ratio of their hypotenuses.
